subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The aim is clearly stated and its relevance to memoryâenhancement programmes for adolescents is well explained.
The multiâstore model is accurately described and explicitly linked to the recency/primacy effects and distractor manipulation.
The hypothesis includes both null and research forms, with IV and DV correctly identified and operationalized.
weaknesses: Minor wording lapses and an incomplete final sentence slightly interrupt flow.
No mention of alternative models to contextualize the MSM link.
Hypothesis wording could be more precise (e.g., specifying âsix recency wordsâ).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All introduction strands were assessed at the top band (5â6) but with minor lapses in precision and completeness. The aimâs relevance is well argued (band 5â6), the MSM link is strong but lacking competingâmodel context (band 5â6) and the hypothesis is operationalized but slightly imprecise (band 5â6). A score of 5/6 reflects this strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: The repeatedâmeasures design is not only described but its advantages (control of individual differences) are explained.
Sampling is justified in terms of accessibility and representativeness for the school population.
Controlled variables and choice of materials are listed with rationale to isolate the distraction effect.
weaknesses: No mention of counterbalancing to mitigate order effects.
Discussion of sampling bias and limitations is limited.
Further justification of materialsâ cultural validity is needed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each exploration strand reached the 3â4 band by offering explanations rather than mere descriptions, but explanations were somewhat surfaceâlevel or incomplete. A score of 3/4 appropriately reflects solid design work with room for deeper methodological justification.

strengths: Both descriptive and inferential statistics are attempted, demonstrating knowledge of appropriate tests.
Findings are interpreted with reference to the hypothesis, showing an understanding of dataâtheory linkage.
weaknesses: Wilcoxon test is miscalculated (incorrect N) and lacks full justification.
Graphical presentation omits axis labels, error bars and a clear title.
Use of mean/SD on ordinal data is questionable; nonâparametric descriptives would be more appropriate.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis shows effort in statistics and interpretation (bands 3â4 and 5â6) but contains fundamental calculation and presentation errors that keep it in the middle band. A conservative score of 3/6 acknowledges partial successes while reflecting serious flaws.

strengths: Findings are discussed in depth with reference to the MSM, Glanzer & Cunitz and contemporary research (e.g., Sasaki).
Strengths and limitations are clearly stated, explained and directly linked to the investigation.
Modifications are explicitly tied to identified limitations and well justified.
weaknesses: Discussion occasionally overstates adolescent STM robustness without sufficient caution.
Integration of proposed modifications with each theoretical point could be tighter.
Empirical feasibility of some modifications could be elaborated further.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All evaluation strands fall into the top band, showing wellâjustified discussion, linked strengths/limitations and modifications. Minor issues in depth and balance prevent the perfect 6, so a score of 5/6 accurately reflects this strong evaluative work.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph that contrasts conditions is provided and addresses the hypothesis, but lacks titles on axes, dispersion indicators and has low resolution. Those errors align with middle band (graph addresses hypothesis but contains errors). Score 3.

The student states statistical outcome (non-significant W), links it to acceptance of the null, and interprets the mean/SD pattern relative to the hypothesis. Interpretation is coherent and explicitly tied to data and hypothesis. Minor misreporting of sample size stops a perfect 6; band 5-6, score 5.

Both null and research hypotheses are given. IV (presence/absence of 30-s delay) and DV (percentage of recency-position words recalled) are **clearly identified and operationalised**. Operationalisation is concrete (six last words; 30-s backward count), satisfying the highest descriptor, though some wording imprecision (âfirst and last few itemsâ in null) prevents a perfect 6.

Sampling is identified as convenience; the student justifies using peers (access, representativeness for school population) and notes gender/age spread and rationale for adolescent focus. This is an **explanation** rather than mere description, but limited consideration of sampling bias keeps it at the lower end of band 3-4.

The multi-store model is **accurately described (sensory, STM, LTM, capacities, transfer mechanisms)** and the student explicitly links recency/primacy effects and the predicted impact of a 30-s distractor to those stores. The connection is logical and well developed, though brief referencing to competing models is absent. Quality comfortably meets band 5-6, meriting 5.

Findings are **discussed at length** in light of MSM, recency theory, Glanzer & Cunitz, and newer studies (Sasaki). The discussion explores why results diverged and theoretical implications. Some depth is sacrificed by overstating adolescent STM robustness, so 5 fits within top band.

The student clearly states the aim â to replicate Glanzer & Cunitzâs recency-effect experiment with adolescents â and **explicitly argues why this matters** (developmental stage, educational implications). The explanation of relevance goes beyond a simple mention, showing how results could inform memory-enhancement programmes. Minor wording lapses do not detract from a well-explained relevance, hence a strong performance within the top band, but not flawless enough for the full 6.

Several strengths (control of individual differences, simple procedure) and limitations (practice effects, limited generalisability, low ecological validity) are **explained and linked** to the investigation. Coverage is relevant and balanced, though not exhaustive; band 5-6, score 5.

Controlled variables (word familiarity, environment, presentation speed) and materials (lists, slideshow) are listed **with rationale** showing how each controls extraneous influence. Some controls are asserted rather than evidenced, but overall choice is explained, fitting band 3-4; awarded 3.

Proposed modifications (counterbalancing, multi-session design, broader sampling, realistic distraction task) are **explicitly tied to corresponding limitations** and justified. Reasoning is clear; while empirical feasibility isnât elaborated, linkage and justification meet 5-6 band; score 5.

The student not only describes but **explains** why a repeated-measures design was chosen (controls individual differences, raises practice-effect risk). Explanation is present but somewhat surface-level; no mention of statistical power or specific threats handled. This matches the lower half of the 3-4 band, so 3.

Both descriptive and inferential statistics are attempted; however, the Wilcoxon calculation is flawed (mis-states N=8, ignores 27 pairs) and mean difference exposition is minimal. Errors place it in 3-4 band rather than top. Given effort and partial correctness, 3 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: Sampling through convenience is justified by representativeness and accessibility. This explanation shows understanding of participant selection implications.
The multi-store model is described accurately, including store capacities and transfer mechanisms. This demonstrates strong theoretical understanding underpinning the investigation.
Link the model description explicitly to your aims by adding a sentence explaining how the MSM predicts changes in the recency effect with a 30 s distractor in adolescents.
The review of Glanzer & Cunitzâs (1966) procedure is clear, but specifying group sizes (e.g., n per condition) would improve clarity and contextualize their findings.
The final sentence of this paragraph is incomplete. Ensure the aim statement is fully presented to maintain coherence in your introductionâs flow.
Your research hypothesis identifies IV and DV but uses âfirst and last few itemsâ instead of the specified six recency words. Operationalize quantitatively to strengthen precision.
The paragraph on applied relevance is well argued, linking findings to educational programmes for adolescents. This underlines your investigationâs societal value.
Ethical adherence is clearly explained, covering consent, withdrawal rights and confidentiality. This fulfills ethical requirements for human research.
The discussion of SD consistency is descriptive but doesnât link to the hypothesis. Explain how tighter variance under noâtask condition supports your expectations.
The operational definitions of IV and DV are clear, but the null hypothesis refers to the test rather than stating no difference in recency recall percentages. Rephrase accordingly.
You explain the repeatedâmeasures designâs benefit for individual differences. To enhance, mention counterbalancing or session separation to mitigate order/practice effects.
Justifying mean and standard deviation on ordinal data is weak; ordinal recency scores often require median and IQR. Consider using nonâparametric descriptive measures.
Table 1 lacks clear axis units. Label columns to specify âMean words recalledâ per recency section (out of 6) to enhance clarity and selfâsufficiency.
Controlled variables (familiarity, noise, speed) are listed with rationale. This demonstrates thoughtful design to isolate the effect of the distraction task.
Materials description is adequate. You could further justify the monosyllabic word generatorâs validity and cultural appropriateness for your adolescent sample.
The detailed procedural description with break intervals enhances reproducibility. Including specific timings improves methodological transparency.
You report the mean difference of 0.68 but omit the calculation. Including the subtraction formula $$3.31 - 2.63 = 0.68$$ would improve transparency.
Proposing a more ecological distraction task (e.g., math problems) is relevant and improves ecological validity. This shows thoughtful consideration of realâworld applicability.
The evaluation restates results but could better integrate with proposed modifications by linking each change to a specific limitation for coherence.
The bar graph addresses the hypothesis but lacks axis labels, error bars, and clear title. Include labels and dispersion indicators to meet topâband graphical standards.
Justify use of the Wilcoxon test by emphasising that recency scores are ordinal ranks, not interval measures. This reinforces appropriateness of inferential choice.
The sample size N=8 is miscalculated. Nonâzero pairs should equal number of participants with differences (n=27), not 8. Correct N to ensure accurate Wâvalue inference.
Summarize your inferential outcome succinctly and link it directly to the hypothesis: state that the Wilcoxon Wâvalue did not fall below critical, thus supporting null acceptance.
Link your discussion back to the MSM by explicitly noting that nonâsignificance suggests STM retention may transfer to LTM under distraction, challenging model assumptions.
Your discussion integrates Sasaki (2009) well to explain adolescent STM resilience. This demonstrates effective synthesis of contemporary research.
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations, such as using counterbalancing to reduce practice effects. This demonstrates high justification and relevance.
